findings.topic: Plan 9: every resource is a file hierarchy spoken over one wire protocol (9P)
findings.summary: Plan 9 is built on exactly three principles: 'resources are named and accessed like files in a hierarchical file system', a single standard protocol (9P) for accessing them, and per-process assembly of disjoint hierarchies into one private namespace. 9P has 17 message types: 3 for initializing/authenticating a connection (auth, attach) and 14 for manipulating objects (clone, walk, stat/wstat, open, read, write, create, remove, clunk). 9P 'controls file systems, not just files' — it includes name resolution and hierarchy traversal; the client's namespace is held client-side, not on the server. The kernel-resident 'mount device' is the SOLE RPC bridge between local procedure calls and remote/user-level 9P servers — no stub compiler, just a shared message-packing library. Whether a service is provided 'by the kernel, a user process, or a remote server is irrelevant to the way it is used'. The full SGI multiprocessor kernel was 25,000 lines of C; a minimal functional kernel ~6,000 lines. The 'names' paper explicitly frames it for OO readers: 'files are similar to objects, except that files are already provided with naming, access, and protection methods that must be created afresh for objects'.
findings.designImplication: For an agent kernel, this argues for ONE kernel-level resource protocol (a 9P-descendant with ~15-20 message types) under which every agent-visible resource — tiered memory stores, tools, model endpoints, other agents — is a mountable synthetic hierarchy served indifferently by kernel, userspace daemon, or remote node. This collapses the MCP/tool-adapter zoo into the mount table: a remote vLLM server, a local llama.cpp, and the Claude API all appear as identically-shaped served trees (e.g. /model/<name>/ctl, /model/<name>/completions), making LLM-agnosticism a property of the namespace rather than of an SDK. Keep the protocol byte/text-oriented: textual synthetic files are natively LLM-parseable, so 'cat' becomes the universal agent introspection verb without bespoke tool schemas.

findings.topic: Plan 9: per-process namespaces, bind/mount/union directories as the composition primitive
findings.summary: Each process has a private rooted namespace; rfork takes 'a bit vector defining which attributes of the process are to be shared between parent and child instead of copied', namespace included. mount(fd, old, flags) attaches a 9P service's tree at a directory; bind(new, old, flags) re-projects existing namespace; flags place the new tree before/after/replacing existing contents, producing 'union directories' searched in order — /bin is a union of /$cputype/bin, /rc/bin and private bins, which replaces $PATH entirely. There is 'no global name space' but 'local name spaces that obey globally understood conventions'. import grafts remote subtrees ('import helix /proc' makes remote processes debuggable locally; 'import helix /net' provides transparent network gatewaying); cpu inverts it, re-creating the terminal's namespace on a fast server. The window system 8½ serves a fresh /dev/cons and /dev/bitblt per window, so it can run recursively inside its own windows; iostats wraps a process in an interposed namespace and accounts every 9P request it makes. A self-admitted gap: a namespace cannot be serialized and shipped — 'it has no mechanism to give the description of a process's name space to another process except by direct inheritance'.
findings.designImplication: Per-agent namespaces should be the kernel-default isolation and composition unit: spawning a sub-agent = constructing a namespace (these tools, this memory tier, this model endpoint) rather than passing a config blob. Union directories give layered capability ergonomics for free: bind a session-scratch memory tier over a persistent tier and reads search the union in order — a natural kernel mechanism for the OS's default tiered memory. Interposition (iostats-style) means audit/budget/guardrail proxies are just file servers mounted between agent and resource — self-improvement services can observe every resource access without instrumenting the agent. Fix Plan 9's admitted gap at the kernel level: namespaces must be first-class, serializable, diffable objects so an orchestrator agent can snapshot, transmit, and replay a sub-agent's exact resource view.

findings.topic: Plan 9: synthetic textual file servers (/proc, /net, /dev/cons) as the introspection/control surface
findings.summary: The process device /proc has one directory per process with files: mem (readable/writable VM image), text (executable + symbols), ctl (write textual 'stop'/'kill'), status (fixed-format text line: name, owner, state), note (textual signals). 'cat /proc/*/status' is a crude ps; the real ps 'merely reformats the data so obtained'. Networks are files: /net/tcp/clone allocates a connection directory; writing the text 'connect 135.104.53.2!512' to ctl then reading/writing data implements a client — 'an rlogin service can be implemented in a few lines of shell code'. Console device files /dev/time, /dev/cputime, /dev/pid, /dev/user 'contain text, not binary numbers, so their use is free of byte-order problems', synthesized on demand when read. The papers stress these services are 'easy to build, easy to understand, and easy to use' and come 'with agreed-upon rules for protection, naming, and access both local and remote'.
findings.designImplication: The agent kernel should ship a default synthetic tree per agent, e.g. /agent/<id>/{status,ctl,context,goals,memory/{episodic,semantic,working},inbox,trace,budget}: status as a fixed-format text line, ctl accepting textual verbs ('pause', 'compact-context', 'checkpoint'), inbox as an append-only message file. Multi-agent coordination then needs no new IPC API — agent A reads /agent/B/status and writes /agent/B/inbox, and a supervisor's 'ps' is cat over the union. Textual ctl files are the right ABI for LLMs (models emit text natively, no marshalling layer), and the /net/tcp/clone allocation pattern (read clone -> get numbered resource directory) is the right kernel idiom for allocating sessions, model conversations, and memory transactions.

findings.topic: Plan 9: where the file metaphor deliberately stops — process creation and shared memory
findings.summary: The names paper's 'Position' section records two deliberate exclusions. Process creation is NOT a write to /proc: 'the details of constructing the environment of the new process — its open files, name space, memory image, etc. — are too intricate to be described easily in a simple I/O operation', so rfork/exec remain syscalls and '/proc is used only to represent and control existing processes'. Shared memory is also excluded: 'Memory is controlled by system calls rather than special files... since a representation in the file system would imply that memory could be imported from remote machines.' Network address spaces likewise resisted uniform mapping because 'the various mechanisms to authenticate, select a service, and control the connection would not map consistently into operations on a file'.
findings.designImplication: A greenfield agent kernel should adopt the same honesty test: every subsystem is file-served by default, but operations whose semantics are intricate-constructor-like (spawning an agent with a full namespace, memory image, and budget) or bandwidth/latency-critical (sharing a KV-cache or vector index between colocated agents, GPU mappings) get dedicated typed syscalls, with /proc-style files only for representation and control after the fact. Concretely: agent_spawn(manifest) as a syscall returning a handle, plus /agent/<id>/ for inspection; mmap-style sharing of embedding/KV regions as a local-only kernel primitive that is deliberately not remotable, while the file/9P layer remains the remotable plane.

findings.topic: KeyKOS: capability nanokernel + universal persistence (orthogonal checkpointing) in production since 1983
findings.summary: KeyKOS is 'a capability-based object-oriented operating system in production use since 1983' (Tymnet hosts): nanokernel ~20,000 lines of C plus <2,000 lines of assembler, runs in under 100 KB of memory, with 'intervals between failures in excess of one year'. Three foundations: stateless kernel (no critical state held in kernel; all state derivable from persistent store), single-level store ('all data lives in persistent virtual memory. Only the nanokernel is aware of the distinction between main memory and disk pages'), and capabilities ('objects are exclusively invoked through protected capabilities'). System-wide checkpoints at tunable intervals; 'on restart, all processes are restored to their exact state at the time of checkpoint, including registers and virtual memory', restart typically <30 seconds; 'several IBM 4341 systems ran for more than three years across power failures without a logical interruption of service' — a power failure appears to applications 'only as an unexplained jump in the value of the real time clock'. Six fundamental kernel objects: devices, pages, nodes (exactly 16 keys each — all capabilities live in nodes), segments, meters (CPU resource control), domains (processes). Messages have copy semantics and are never buffered by the kernel; 'factories' create domains whose trustworthiness (leak-freedom) a client can verify; 'space banks' meter storage hierarchically.
findings.designImplication: This is the strongest precedent for the project's 'default persistent tiered memory' mandate: make persistence orthogonal and kernel-level, not an agent-implemented save/load protocol. An agent (context, scratchpad, capability set, in-flight task state) should survive power failure by default via system-wide checkpoint, with restart measured in seconds — turning long-horizon agent tasks from 'resumable if the framework cooperates' into a kernel guarantee. KeyKOS meters map directly to token/compute budgets as first-class kernel objects attached to every agent; space banks map to hierarchical memory-tier quotas; factories map to verified agent templates whose confinement (cannot leak data to their creator) is checkable before instantiation — exactly what running untrusted third-party agents requires.

findings.topic: EROS lineage: why ACLs structurally fail at 'limiting' and 'granting' — the ambient authority critique
findings.summary: Shapiro's EROS essay defines a capability as 'a token [that] designates an object and gives the program the authority to perform a specific set of actions' and identifies four access-control goals: preventing, limiting, granting, revoking. His core claim: 'ACL systems can prevent and revoke access, but they can neither limit access nor grant access' — every program running as a user wields the user's full ambient authority ('that raging inferno program written by a stranger in Norway' can read all the user's files). 'Access control is about determining what a program can access, not about what a person can access.' A UNIX fd 'is essentially a capability'. The historical blocker for capability systems was persistence of the capability graph ('the main problem with capabilities is finding a way to save them to disk'), solved by universal persistence: 'every five minutes, write down all of the things the computer is working on' — used by KeyKOS and EROS, and claimed 'actually faster than file systems and requires less code'. Capability systems also enable mutually-suspicious collaboration (run my secret program on your secret data, neither side leaks) which 'in ACL systems... really is impossible'; selective revocation is handled by interposed compartments/severing. 'You can't build a capability system on top of an ACL system', but UNIX compatibility environments have been built on top of capability systems.
findings.designImplication: Agents are precisely the 'programs, not people' actors of Shapiro's critique — an agent-native kernel must have NO ambient authority at any layer: no default filesystem root, no default network, no implicit model-API key inheritance; every tool call, memory access, and model invocation is exercised through an explicit capability. Per-user identity (the POSIX uid model) should not exist as the kernel security primitive at all. The mutually-suspicious-collaboration property is the kernel feature that makes an agent marketplace viable (proprietary agent x confidential user data, provably no exfiltration). And Shapiro's directionality result fixes the layering: capabilities in the kernel, POSIX/ACL compatibility as an optional userspace personality on top — never the reverse.

findings.topic: Capsicum: hybrid capability retrofit — cap_enter + fd rights masks, with concrete adoption-cost numbers
findings.summary: Capsicum (USENIX Security 2010; shipped experimental in FreeBSD 9) adds two kernel primitives to POSIX: capability mode (a process credential flag set by cap_enter; one-way, inherited; 'cuts off the sandbox's access to global namespaces' — filesystem paths, PIDs, IPv4/ports, SysV/POSIX IPC, sysctl are blocked or constrained) and capabilities (refined file descriptors with 'roughly 60 possible mask rights', checked in the kernel's fget descriptor-lookup; delegation via fork/exec inheritance or UNIX domain socket passing; re-derivation can only subset rights). Process descriptors replace PIDs with capability-style handles. Measured adoption costs: tcpdump sandboxed in a 2-line change (cap_enter before the pcap loop; ~10 lines with lc_limitfd on stdio); gzip incurs 'a constant-time penalty of 2.4 ms' per run; Chromium needed ~100 lines vs 'hundreds of lines of code or policy to, in one case, 22,000 lines of C++' for other platforms' sandboxes; capability-aware syscalls are 'just a few percent slower' than UNIX equivalents. Failures return a distinct errno ENOTCAPABLE, and open() is blocked deep in namei so violations are visible in ktrace/DTrace — deliberately supporting incremental compartmentalization of existing code.
findings.designImplication: Two transferable lessons. (1) Enforce rights at the kernel's single object-lookup chokepoint (Capsicum's fget pattern): in an agent kernel every handle dereference — tool descriptor, memory-tier handle, model-session handle — carries a rights mask checked in one place, so the security kernel stays small and auditable. (2) Capsicum's value was an incremental path for legacy code; a greenfield kernel doesn't need cap_enter (everything is born in capability mode), but should keep the diagnostics design: a dedicated ENOTCAPABLE-style error channel plus kernel-level tracing of denied accesses, because for agents these denials are also the feedback signal a self-improvement service mines to propose capability-manifest adjustments. POSIX-compat personalities, if offered, should run pre-entered into capability mode by default.

findings.topic: Fuchsia/Zircon: handles = {object, rights, owner}; monotonic attenuation; handles move through channels
findings.summary: A Zircon handle is the only way usermode references kernel objects: in-kernel it is three fields — 'a reference to a kernel object, the rights to the kernel object, the process it is bound to (or... the kernel)' (in-transit). Usermode sees a per-process 32-bit integer (0 = ZX_HANDLE_INVALID; valid handles always have the two least-significant bits set). Lifecycle syscalls: creation calls (zx_event_create, zx_process_create, ...) return the first handle with type-default rights; zx_handle_duplicate copies (requires ZX_RIGHT_DUPLICATE, may drop rights); zx_handle_replace produces an equivalent-or-lesser handle invalidating the original; zx_channel_write puts handles in-transit, zx_channel_read/call rebind them — the only way to transfer authority between processes; zx_process_start gives a new process exactly one bootstrapping handle 'that it can use to request other handles' (canonically one end of a channel). Kernel objects are refcounted by handles, so a valid handle implies a live object. Rights (~24 defined in zircon/rights.h) are fine-grained: DUPLICATE, TRANSFER, READ, WRITE, EXECUTE, MAP, GET/SET_PROPERTY, ENUMERATE, DESTROY, GET/SET_POLICY, SIGNAL, SIGNAL_PEER, WAIT, INSPECT, APPLY_PROFILE, RESIZE, MANAGE_VMO, ...; ZX_RIGHTS_BASIC = DUPLICATE|TRANSFER|WAIT|INSPECT. Job policy ZX_POL_BAD_HANDLE + ZX_POL_ACTION_EXCEPTION turns bad-handle use into an exception for hygiene enforcement.
findings.designImplication: Zircon is the closest production blueprint for the agent kernel's object layer: agents, memory tiers, model sessions, tool connections, and budgets as refcounted kernel objects reachable only via rights-bearing handles, with attenuation-only duplication (an orchestrator dupes its memory handle as read-only before handing it to a researcher sub-agent) and channel-mediated transfer giving an auditable, kernel-observable authority-flow graph — raw material for the self-improvement service to learn least-privilege manifests. Adopt the single-bootstrap-handle birth protocol verbatim: a freshly spawned agent holds exactly one channel and must be granted everything else, making its whole authority set enumerable at spawn time. Define agent-semantic rights in the mask (e.g. RIGHT_INVOKE_MODEL, RIGHT_SPAWN_AGENT, RIGHT_WRITE_LONGTERM_MEMORY, RIGHT_RECALL) parallel to READ/WRITE/MAP, enforced kernel-level.

findings.topic: Fuchsia namespaces: per-component composite hierarchies reconcile Plan 9 naming with object capabilities
findings.summary: A Fuchsia namespace is 'a composite hierarchy of files, directories, sockets, services, devices' assembled per component: 'Fuchsia does not have a root filesystem'; 'there is no global root namespace'; 'every component effectively has its own private root'. At start a component receives 'a table that maps one or more namespace path prefixes to object handles' (Namespace Transfer). Crucially, namespaces and capabilities are unified: 'To access an object within a namespace, you must already have another object in your possession... you can only access objects that are reachable from the ones you already have access to. There is no ambient authority.' FIDL protocol services are namespace entries — fuchsia.logger.Log lives at /svc/fuchsia.logger.Log — so 'the mechanisms used to control access to files can also be used to control access to services'. Conventional layout: pkg/ (read-only package), data/ (private persistent), tmp/, svc/ (offered protocols), dev/ (privileged only), config/. Names are 1-255 bytes, paths max 4095 bytes, no .. traversal at protocol level (client-side canonicalization only); a filesystem is 'simply a component that publishes file-like objects'; components publish their own outgoing directory into others' namespaces.
findings.designImplication: This resolves the apparent Plan 9-vs-capabilities tension for the agent kernel: keep Plan 9's discoverable, ls-able hierarchical naming as the agent-facing surface, but make every directory entry a transferred handle so the namespace IS the capability set — path resolution never mints new authority. Adopt the prefix-table birth contract: an agent manifest declares prefixes (/memory/episodic, /tools/web, /model/default, /agents/peers/...) and the kernel hands the corresponding handles at spawn; what is absent from the table is unreachable, period. Banning .. at the protocol level eliminates the entire path-traversal class of prompt-injection exploits (an injected '../../secrets' is unrepresentable). The svc/ convention shows how typed agent protocols (A2A-style) and plain files coexist in one tree; default layout for agents should be standardized exactly as Fuchsia standardized pkg/data/tmp/svc.

findings.topic: Redox OS: URL/scheme-addressed resources served by registered userspace daemons over a Rust microkernel
findings.summary: Redox names resources by URI-style schemes: a scheme 'identifies the type of resource and identifies the manager daemon responsible for that resource' and is 'the root of the path to the resource'. Daemons register a scheme name with the kernel at boot; File::create("/scheme/tcp/127.0.0.1/3000") is converted by the kernel into open("127.0.0.1/3000") delivered to the 'tcp' scheme provider, which parses the path, opens the connection, and returns a handle. Scheme providers define what file operations mean ('seek to an SSD driver scheme might simply add to a file offset, but to a floppy disk controller... physical movement of read-write heads'); fmap provides shared-memory mapping between scheme provider and consumer (used for device MMIO and framebuffers). The split is explicit policy: ~28 userspace schemes (file/redoxfs, tcp/udp/ip/icmp via smolnetd, display.*, audio, pty, rand, null, zero, chan for IPC, shm, sudo as a privilege-manager scheme, ...) versus only ~14 kernel schemes (namespace, user-scheme dispatch, debug, event, irq, pipe, proc, memory, time, itimer, serio) — 'when possible, schemes should be implemented in userspace', kernel schemes reserved for 'critical resources not easily managed with a userspace daemon'.
findings.designImplication: Redox demonstrates the pluggability mechanism the agent kernel needs: the kernel owns only scheme registration, dispatch, and a minimal critical set (memory, events, IPC, agent contexts), while every resource type is a swappable userspace daemon. Map this directly: memory:, vector:, model:, tool:, agent:, trace: schemes where 'model:anthropic/...' and 'model:local/llama' are two daemons behind one scheme contract — LLM-agnosticism becomes 'which daemon registered the scheme', and the OS's DEFAULT tiered memory is simply the memory-scheme daemon that ships in the base image with users free to register alternatives. The typed-scheme root (vs Plan 9's pure mount-point convention) also gives agents machine-readable resource typing for free. Keep Redox's fmap escape hatch: scheme daemons that need bandwidth (vector indexes, KV caches, GPU buffers) hand out shared mappings instead of read/write streams.

findings.topic: WebAssembly nanoprocess model + WASI: deny-by-default, library-granularity isolation with explicit authority plumbing
findings.summary: The Bytecode Alliance's founding article defines the 'nanoprocess': process-like isolation 'much lighter weight' where cross-boundary calls are 'not much slower than a regular function call'. Mechanisms: (1) each Wasm module is sandboxed by default — 'the module doesn't have access to APIs and system calls... you have to explicitly provide the module with the function or syscall'; (2) linear-memory encapsulation — a module 'only has access to the chunk of memory that has been assigned to it'; (3) interface types let the engine 'do direct copies between the caller and the callee's memories, without having to serialize and deserialize'; (4) WASI bakes permissions into the system interface ('fine-grained permissions to particular parts of the file system... and for different system calls'). Authority changes are structurally loud: a module asking for new imports changes its signature, so 'there's no way to sneak in access to a new resource or system call under the radar' — motivated by supply-chain data (80% of average codebases from registries; npm malicious modules more than doubled 2017-2019; the electron-native-notify wallet heist needed only a socket + memory access). Declared missing piece: per-module virtualization to pass attenuated authority down dependency trees. Production proof point: Fastly 'securely host[s] tens of thousands of simultaneously running programs in the same process' with cold start 'orders of magnitude faster'. Explicit caveat: 'CPUs and OSes don't offer finer-than-process granularity time protection', so Spectre-class side channels still require OS processes.
findings.designImplication: Nanoprocesses are the right default execution grain for agent TOOLS and skills: an agent kernel can host thousands of tool instances per host process with near-function-call dispatch latency — compatible with agents that compose dozens of tools per reasoning step, where per-tool VMs or containers are cost-prohibitive. The import-signature property gives the kernel a static, diffable authority manifest per tool, which the self-improvement service can tighten over time, and 'tool asks for new imports' becomes a kernel-mediated consent event rather than a silent upgrade. But honor the article's own side-channel caveat at kernel level: mutually distrusting agents (different principals/tenants) get separate hardware-backed protection domains; nanoprocess isolation is the intra-agent default, not the inter-tenant boundary.

findings.topic: Wasm Component Model: typed WIT contracts, no exported memory, statically analyzable composition graphs
findings.summary: Components extend core Wasm modules (which can only pass i32/i64/f32/f64, forcing strings to be (offset,length) pairs into convention-named exported memories with 'nothing in the type system to prevent the returned length from being confused with the returned offset'). A component is a distinct binary format (version 0x1000d vs core 0x1) 'restricted to interact only through the modules' imported and exported functions', with interfaces in WIT (strings, lists, records, variants, nesting) and bit-level representation fixed by the Canonical ABI. Two security-relevant properties: 'a component may not export a memory and thus it cannot indirectly communicate to others by writing to its memory' — enabling GC-language and linear-memory components to interoperate; and 'the relationships within a graph of components can be statically analysed: for example, to verify that a component containing business logic has no access to a component containing personally identifiable information'. Components compose into larger graphs with one component's exports satisfying another's imports, language-blind ('a component implemented in Go can communicate directly and safely with a C or Rust component').
findings.designImplication: Adopt WIT-style typed interfaces as the kernel's canonical tool/skill ABI: tool contracts expressed in an interface language with rich types kills the (offset,length)-style ambiguity that plagues stringly-typed tool calling, and the no-exported-memory rule guarantees tools share state only through kernel-visible channels — every byte that moves between tools is interceptable, traceable, and policy-checkable. The static-analysis property is the killer feature for agent ecosystems: before an agent loads a third-party skill graph, the kernel can PROVE 'the web-search component has no path to the credentials component' — a compile-time complement to runtime capability checks. Implication for layering: component linking/verification is a kernel-adjacent trusted service (default), while individual component runtimes (Wasmtime, WAMR-class) remain pluggable userspace engines.

findings.topic: gVisor: a user-space application kernel (Sentry) with 9P-mediated I/O — the cost profile of syscall interposition
findings.summary: gVisor is 'an application kernel that implements a Linux-like interface', written in Go, running in userspace — 'neither a syscall filter... nor a VM' but 'a merged guest kernel and VMM, or seccomp on steroids'. Per sandbox: the Sentry implements syscalls, signal delivery, memory management, page-fault logic and threading, and 'does not pass system calls through to the host kernel'; the Sentry itself runs in a restricted seccomp container with no filesystem access, and a per-container Gofer process mediates all file access 'via the 9P protocol over a socket or shared memory channel' (defense-in-depth: even a compromised Sentry has no direct FS reach). Syscall interception is platform-pluggable (ptrace universal/slowest, KVM, Systrap recommended). Measured cost structure ('structural' vs 'implementation' costs, n1-standard-4 GCE benchmarks): raw memory access and CPU-bound work have no added overhead (sysbench CPU parity; TensorFlow CNN training near-parity); raw-syscall latency is the dominant structural cost and varies strongly by platform (ptrace vs KVM vs native runc); memory density tests run 50 containers (5 for redis) showing 'a small, mostly fixed' per-sandbox overhead; small-op redis and static-file apache (100k file, ApacheBench) are the pathological cases via syscall + VFS serialization costs.
findings.designImplication: Three takeaways for a greenfield design. (1) gVisor exists because Linux's syscall surface is too big to trust — an agent-native kernel that starts with a small capability/handle syscall set never needs an interposition layer; build the narrow interface first instead of wrapping a wide one. (2) The Sentry/Gofer split validates a two-tier trust architecture worth copying natively: per-agent supervisor logic isolated from the I/O-authority holder, connected by an explicit protocol (notably 9P again — converging with the Plan 9 finding). (3) The cost data sets the design budget: agent workloads are dominated by model inference (CPU/GPU-bound, ~zero isolation overhead) plus bursts of tool I/O (syscall-bound, the expensive part) — so optimize the kernel's I/O path (shared-memory channels, batched/async submission a la the Gofer shared-memory option) and accept interposition-style indirection only where untrusted native binaries must run, as an optional compatibility personality rather than the default execution path.

openQuestions: seL4 was not covered here: does formal verification of the capability kernel (seL4's proofs, its CNode/CSpace model and IPC fastpath numbers) change the recommended kernel core, and is verification realistic for a greenfield agent kernel's handle layer?

9P's chatty walk/open/read sequence performs poorly over high-latency links (known from 9P-over-WAN experience and gVisor's move from 9P to LISAfs-style protocols); what latency budget does an agent-resource protocol need when memory tiers and model endpoints are remote, and does it require batched/pipelined or cache-coherent extensions?

Capabilities are unforgeable kernel tokens, but agents act through generated text: what is the binding between a token an LLM emits in a tool call and a kernel handle, and how does the kernel prevent confused-deputy attacks where prompt-injected text causes an agent to exercise a capability it legitimately holds (rights masks limit blast radius but do not encode intent)?

KeyKOS-style universal persistence checkpoints the capability graph atomically; how does that interact with revocation and with external non-transactional resources (a restored agent holds handles to model sessions and remote APIs that did not roll back)?

The Bytecode Alliance explicitly concedes no sub-process time protection (Spectre-class side channels): for colocated mutually-distrusting agents sharing one inference server or KV-cache, what is the practical placement policy — and do GPU-side channels need their own isolation story?

Plan 9's admitted gap (namespaces not serializable/transmittable) and Fuchsia's component manifests both gesture at a declarative agent-spawn manifest format; which existing schema (Fuchsia .cml, OCI runtime spec, WASI component imports) is the right starting point for the agent manifest, or is a new one justified?

Redox's scheme registration is kernel-mediated by name; how should scheme/handle namespaces federate across machines in a multi-node agent swarm — Plan 9 import-style mounting, A2A-style discovery, or capability-passing over network channels (and what replaces 9P auth/attach for inter-kernel trust)?

Per-module virtualization ('passing keys down the dependency tree') was flagged as unfinished by the Bytecode Alliance in 2019; what is the current state of WASI 0.2/0.3 resource handles and does the component model now provide attenuated re-export natively?
